title: Recursion in DSA
description: Going beyond Recursion Basics -- multiple recursive calls (Fibonacci), the repeated-work problem it exposes, and a first taste of memoization.
objectives: Write a recursive function with more than one recursive call
Recognize repeated work in naive multi-call recursion
Fix it with memoization using a HashMap

sections.text: In Recursion Basics, factorial(n) made exactly ONE recursive call per invocation. Many DSA problems make MULTIPLE recursive calls -- the classic example is the Fibonacci sequence, where each number is the sum of the two before it.
fib(6) = fib(5) + fib(4) = ... = 8, following the sequence 0,1,1,2,3,5,8,13... Each call now branches into TWO more calls (except the base case), unlike factorial's single chain.

sections.title: Partial call tree for fib(5)
steps: fib(4) calls fib(3) and fib(2) -- notice fib(3) is called AGAIN
fib(3) (from fib(5)'s side) calls fib(2) and fib(1) -- fib(2) is called AGAIN too
fib(2) gets recomputed from scratch every single time it's needed, even though the answer never changes

sections.text: This naive fib is O(2^n) -- exponential! The same sub-problems (like fib(2) or fib(3)) get recalculated from scratch, repeatedly, instead of being reused. For fib(40), this becomes genuinely too slow to run in reasonable time.
Caching the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again, instead of recomputing them.

sections.title: How memoization fixes it
points: Before computing fibMemo(n), check memo (a HashMap, from the Hashing lesson) -- if we've already solved it, return the cached answer instantly
Otherwise compute it normally, but SAVE the result into memo before returning
Each distinct n is now computed only ONCE -- turning O(2^n) into O(n)

sections.text: fibMemo(30) would take a very long time with the naive version, but returns nearly instantly here -- a dramatic, concrete demonstration of memoization's power. This exact idea -- caching sub-problem results -- is the foundation of Dynamic Programming, covered as its own full module (Greedy & Dynamic Programming) later.
4. Multiple Recursive Calls: Generating All Subsets (Preview)

This is only a PREVIEW of a technique called backtracking -- trying a choice, recursing, then UNDOING that choice (current.remove(...)) to try the next one. Backtracking gets its own full dedicated lesson right after this one, with much more depth.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing multi-call recursion (like naive Fibonacci) without recognizing the exponential repeated-work problem it creates for larger inputs.
Forgetting to check the memo cache BEFORE doing the recursive work, defeating the entire point of memoization.
Forgetting to actually SAVE a freshly computed result into the memo before returning it.
Confusing memoization (caching results, top-down from the original call) with the tabulation approach covered later in Dynamic Programming (building up from the base cases instead).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why is naive recursive Fibonacci O(2^n)?
questions.answer: Because each call (except the base case) branches into two more recursive calls, and the same sub-problems (like fib(2) or fib(3)) get recomputed repeatedly across different branches of the call tree, instead of being reused.

questions.question: What is memoization, and how does it fix naive Fibonacci's performance?
questions.answer: Memoization caches the result of each distinct sub-problem the first time it's computed, and returns that cached result on any later request for the same input -- turning Fibonacci's O(2^n) naive recursion into O(n), since each value from 0 to n is now computed only once.

questions.question: What data structure is typically used for memoization, and why?
questions.answer: A HashMap (or an array, when inputs are small non-negative integers), because it provides O(1) average lookup and insertion (from the Hashing lesson), letting the cache check and update happen quickly.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Beyond single-call recursion like factorial, many DSA problems involve multiple recursive calls per invocation, like naive Fibonacci's fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). This creates exponential O(2^n) time, since the same sub-problems get recomputed repeatedly across the call tree. Memoization fixes this by caching each sub-problem's result (typically in a HashMap) the first time it's solved, and returning the cached value on any later request for the same input -- reducing Fibonacci to O(n). This same caching idea is the foundation of Dynamic Programming, and recursion with multiple branching choices (like generating all subsets) previews the backtracking technique covered next.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Naive multi-call recursion = re-solving the same homework problem over and over because you forgot you already solved it. Memoization = writing the answer in the margin the FIRST time, so you just glance at it next time.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why is naive recursive Fibonacci exponential?
What is memoization, and what data structure commonly implements it?
How does memoization change Fibonacci's time complexity?
What technique does 'try a choice, recurse, undo the choice' preview?

sections.title: Fibonacci Number
sections.problemStatement: The Fibonacci numbers form a sequence where F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n > 1. Given n, calculate F(n).
hints: This is the exact naive fib() function from this lesson's Section 1 -- fine for small n (up to 30), but memoization (Section 3) becomes necessary for larger n.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Naive recursion, exactly as shown in this lesson's opening example.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the recursion call stack

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Memoized recursion using a HashMap cache, exactly as shown in this lesson's Section 3.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the memo map and call stack

sections.title: Pow(x, n)
sections.problemStatement: Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n (x^n).
hints: Multiplying x by itself n times is O(n) -- but x^n can be computed as (x^(n/2))^2, HALVING the problem size each recursive call, like Binary Search's halving idea.
Handle negative n by computing the positive-power result and taking its reciprocal.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Multiply x by itself n times in a simple loop.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fast exponentiation by recursively halving the exponent: x^n = (x^(n/2))^2, with an extra factor of x when n is odd.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) for the recursion call stack

sections.title: Reverse Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the new head. (This lesson emphasizes the RECURSIVE solution; the iterative solution using next/prev pointers is covered fully in the Singly Linked List lesson.)
constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 5000]
hints: Recursively reverse everything AFTER the current node first, then fix up the current node's links -- the base case is a null or single-node list, which is trivially already 'reversed'.

approaches.label: Brute Force (Iterative)
approaches.explanation: Iteratively relink each node's next pointer backward, tracking prev/current -- the approach from the Singly Linked List lesson, shown here for comparison.

approaches.label: Optimal (Recursive)
approaches.explanation: Recursively reverse the rest of the list first, then make the current node the new tail.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the recursion call stack

sections.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
sections.problemStatement: Merge two sorted linked lists list1 and list2 into one sorted list by splicing together their nodes, and return the head of the merged list.
constraints: The number of nodes in both lists is in the range [0, 50]
hints: At each step, whichever list's current node is smaller becomes the next node in the merged result -- the REST of the merge is a smaller version of the exact same problem, a natural recursive fit.

approaches.label: Brute Force (Iterative)
approaches.explanation: Iteratively build the merged list using a dummy head node and a pointer, advancing whichever list has the smaller current value.

approaches.label: Optimal (Recursive)
approaches.explanation: Recursively pick the smaller head, and let it point to the recursively-merged result of everything else.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for the recursion call stack

sections.title: K-th Symbol in Grammar
sections.problemStatement: We build a table of n rows. Row 1 is "0". For each subsequent row, every '0' becomes "01" and every '1' becomes "10". Given n and k, return the k-th symbol (1-indexed) in row n.
hints: Building the entire row for n up to 30 is way too large (2^29 characters) -- instead, work BACKWARD: figure out the PARENT symbol at row n-1 that produced position k, recursively.

Position k in row n comes from position ceil(k/2) in row n-1 -- and it's FLIPPED if k is even and the parent was 1, or... more precisely: if the parent is 0, the pair is "01"; if the parent is 1, the pair is "10". Whether k lands on the first or second half of that pair determines the final flip.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Actually build each row as a String, doubling its length each time, up to row n.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n) -- the row length itself doubles each level
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(2^n) to store the full row

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Recursively find the parent symbol at row n-1, then determine if the current position is a 'flip' of it, without ever building the actual row string.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the recursion call stack
